case_name: Jared Bush, Jr. v. Columbia Medical Center of Arlington Subsidiary, L.P. D/B/A Medical City Arlington and Hca Inc.

summary.brief: Jared Bush, Jr. v. Columbia Medical Center of Arlington Subsidiary, L.P. D/B/A Medical City Arlington and Hca Inc.. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: 35-year-old Ireille Williams-Bush fainted and was taken by ambulance to Medical City Arlington Hospital. She presented with symptoms consistent with a pulmonary embolism: chest pain, shortness of breath, and severe fainting.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The emergency room doctor’s initial impression was that she had suffered a non-ST-elevated myocardial infarction, so the hospital admitted her under that diagnosis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Williams-Bush was later discharged in stable condition with instructions to follow up in two weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Three days after her discharge, she was found in bed, struggling to breathe. She was rushed to the hospital but died that day. An autopsy revealed clotting in her heart and lungs.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Patterson opines that the Hospital breached the standard of care by failing to adopt and enforce such a protocol and that breach was a direct and proximate cause of Williams-Bush’s death.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Patterson

evidence_index.text: We reverse the judgment of the court of appeals and remand to the trial court for further proceedings.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court of Texas

evidence_index.text: The report adequately supported the claim against the hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court of Texas

evidence_index.text: Dr. Patterson filed an amended report setting forth his qualifications and opinions on standard of care, breach, and causation.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Patterson

evidence_index.text: The trial court did not abuse its discretion in overruling the Hospital’s objections to the amended report and denying the Hospital’s motion to dismiss.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court of Texas

evidence_index.text: The amended report satisfies the statutory requirements.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court of Texas

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with symptoms consistent with a pulmonary embolism: chest pain, shortness of breath, and severe fainting.

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of pulmonary embolism
decision_points.prompt: What diagnostic protocol should be implemented?

options.description: Implement Triple Rule Out protocol including CT angiogram
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard protocol to rule out life-threatening conditions like pulmonary embolism

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant proceeded with cardiac catheterization only

expected_action_court.description: Triple Rule Out protocol should have been implemented
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for comprehensive evaluation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to implement comprehensive diagnostic protocol
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Triple Rule Out protocol should have been used to rule out pulmonary embolism

patient_outcome.description: Patient died from a pulmonary embolism

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to implement appropriate diagnostic protocols
Causation established - failure to diagnose pulmonary embolism

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to implement Triple Rule Out protocol
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Protocol would have identified pulmonary embolism

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to ED with symptoms
Patient fainted and was taken to the hospital

allegations.allegation: Failure to implement appropriate diagnostic protocols
Failure to implement appropriate diagnostic protocols

court_findings.finding: Hospital breached standard of care by not implementing protocols
court_findings.legal_significance: Breach led to failure to diagnose pulmonary embolism
